speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah interview with Henry Frankel. Now, do you remember when you first heard about the war breaking out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was-- we supposed to return on September 1 to school when they came over the news-- there were two weeks prior to the outbreak of the war that there were movement of Polish armies from place to place, and armaments, and so on. And we knew that something was going to happen. But we did believe in the strength of the Polish army to such extent that we didn't give a second thought that it will be so quickly devastated. But this was slowly building up in us that World War II is unavoidable. After what happened to Austria, Czechoslovakia, and we knew that Hitler would try to get Poland. And it actually happened. We got the news of the outbreak of the war, of the attack on Poland, early morning hours of the September 1, 1939. And within a few weeks, Lodz was captured and we started to learn how to deal with Germans, who the Germans are, and how to-- what did they expect to do with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when they first came to Lodz? Do you remember when you first saw Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you first saw the German troops coming to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was on the fourth or fifth of September. So very quickly, within a week, I think, they were in Lodz. And with them came the Einsatzgruppen. We didn't know them, who they were. But they were [INAUDIBLE] they already started to set up certain points, demanding certain things from population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like labor and getting rid of-- expecting all the gold, silver, and valuables to be surrendered to Germans under the protect-- under the warning that in case they will be found on the person there will be death penalty without any court. There were limitation of food and people has to stay in line. And of course, Germans incited the public that if there were Jews, to throw them out of the line. They have to go to special stores. And when these people went to special stores, there was not sufficient amount of food left to-- to be distributed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know these things? How did you know that you can only go to special stores?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were announcing these stores-- as long as they had the bread or whatever provision they were selling, then they came out out of stories there's not enough food for the Polish population. Jews have to go to their own local stores and to ask in Jewish community center where the stores exist. The Jewish population mainly was living within a certain district that they could be reached and recognized as a Jewish quarters, where later around the Germans created the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, though, how could somebody tell that you were Jewish? You could just walk into a store.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's some people there that did not look Jewish and they actually could not tell. But there were cases that people could tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: They were wearing special garb like a head--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --or long caftan. And they had beards with locks on the side and wearing [INAUDIBLE] and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go into these stores or only the Jewish stores? Would you only go to the Jewish stores or did you go sometimes to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were fortunate enough that we had many Polish friends who were bringing for us the material to the-- like bread and other provisions-- to our house. However, there were people that were standing in line, Jewish people standing in line to-- to buy bread or other provisions, milk and whatever. And while they were standing on line, they were German who were living in Lodz and they started beating them up. And they had-- we knew that they were local Germans because they were wearing civilian clothing with the armband and the hakenkruis on it. And they were told to suddenly jam into everybody. And we could tell that they were not Germans from Germany but-- and not-- they are not soldiers. They were the local people who want to take advantage of-- of the Jewish population. And that's how it was. They came-- they're brutalizing people. They were beating them up for any reason whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on several occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that? Can you tell us about that, what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I tell-- yes, they came to-- if they have seen that a man and-- elderly man it was standing and he was without any support of-- of the other people, they were brutalizing him and beating him up and taking his cane away and beating him with a cane until the poor man fell down and they had to be taken away to-- to-- ambulance or-- or be taken away by other people and clear out-- out of the way. And there were people begging them to stop beating them and kicking them and brutalizing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was beating the man up?

speaker: interviewee
text: The local German people with these arm bands. And I remember at least three such incidents and-- in different part of Lodz. And these people, of course, were privileged. They had in their lapel, their little hakenkruis with the red circle. And you could tell that they were people who were living in Poland and knew every movement and trick that anybody else could do-- have. And of course, they were working as the German informers. They spoke fluently Polish and understood quite a bit of Yiddish too, because of the similarity of the-- phonetic similarity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak mostly Yiddish? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was speaking mostly Polish. And Yiddish I learned during the times of camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know Yiddish before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to extent that I could communicate. But I could communicate in Hebrew. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when this fellow that you saw got beaten up, this was in the middle of the day? In the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure. Standing-- there were-- there were curfew hours from 5 o'clock in the morning till 5 o'clock in the afternoon. And there were-- Jews were not allowed to walk around any later. And of course, they had to have ID cards that they live in this district or that they are Jews. Without this such thing, they couldn't get any provisions and food or any other help.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the-- were there any German soldiers around or policeman? Would they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But they never-- never stopped these people from beating them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the crowds react?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did bystanders react?

speaker: interviewee
text: They start begging them and offering them watches, whatever, just to stop beating these poor people. And some of the-- were carrying on on the basis of cruelty and being incited and boiling blood, we used to called it. And once we encounter people like this, it was better to clear out the other way-- out of their way, than permit them to start any troubles. And they were looking at some very, very typical Jewish people, or elderly, who could not resist any blows and thing like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any trouble yourself, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: With myself? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was mainly trying to obtain food, or whatever there was any occasion. I did look non-Jewish, would I say, and spoke fluently Polish. And that helped me to get around without any problems. And I was engaging with many of my friends who tried to study. We did not believe that the war will be continuing for so many years. And try to prepare ourself for, perhaps, later studies, when the war will be finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of your teachers around? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We met several of them, especially this teacher, Bunin, who was assistant to Katzenelson. And he was encouraging us to study whatever material we accumulated in school years-- to continue because he said, once you get out of the times of studies, you will simply forget. You will be occupied with other thing. And it's home or work, so-- whenever we, two or three of us, got together, we were studying quite hard. And we had quite a bit of fun that we could have the opportunity to study and enjoy whatever we'd learned until now. And of course they had to be done very carefully. Because these Germans already had a list of people who were sort of well-off. And there were throwing them out of their places of where they were living. And of course, we were asked to leave the place immediately. Take just whatever was possible. But mother in this respect was very, very keen and she was preparing quite bit of stuff to be taken out before the Germans told us to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the war broke out September 1. By the end of October, we already had quite a bit of our stuff from the-- our-- from our place of living out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when-- how this happened? Who came to the house and told you, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: A young SS man came with a piece of paper. What he expected to see when we leave the place. And he know about the China and the crystals and thing like this. Like, somebody would have delivered to him a list of all the goodies that he expect would leave. And he said, in case you're not going to leave it, and play tricks, we'll find you and we'll liquidate you. It's simply as that. And I remember mother said, what good is the fine porcelain China to us when we can't use it. So let's leave it behind. Whatever happened will happen. And that's how it was. We left it-- just took the most important things with us. We did not surrender all the silverware. We did not surrender all-- all the other important things we travel with until we got to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did they give you to go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: 24 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went to the place-- from this part of Lodz to the place of my father's brother. He was living-- don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aaron. He was younger brother of my father. And a very good uncle. He had three sons. And two of them were attending gymnasium with my brother. And the youngest one was still attending public school when I was already in high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Momentarily I can't remember. But one, Sclamek, spelled S-C-L-A-M-E-K, he came to Warsaw. And mother many times took care of him. And the youngest one remained with my uncle in Lodz. And the middle one just took off for Russia. He thought that perhaps in Russia he will be able to survive easier then. Somehow he survive. He joined the Anders' Army, created by the Russians during the war. And when the Anders' Army came to Israel in-- then he too parted with the army-- remained in the army. And he married over there, but I couldn't trace him down anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father's business at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was taken over by the local Germans who knew about father's business and as a association and the procedure of conducting the [INAUDIBLE]. Many of them were working for us as masters of the certain divisions. And they just took it over there. There was no place of-- they did not even allow father to come pick up his books or whatever important information he had in office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who those people were, any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that before or after you left your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was before. There was a week or two weeks after the Germans entered the-- Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did-- How did that affect your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was totally destroyed to a point that he would never believe the Germans would ever conduct themself in a way like this, because they did remember Germans from the First World War, where they were conducting business with them. And very good-- they were very good friends. Couldn't understand how suddenly these same people, speaking the same language, in the same cultural background, could come to you and say, get out as soon as you can for your own sake. And that was it. He became very, very depressed. And even when we arranged for him the escape from Lodz, he was hesitating. And unfortunately, he could not survive the escape from Lodz to Warsaw that was arranged and prevail. He died Lodz and died on September 1, 1940, exactly a year after the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you stayed in Lodz by your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, until we departed for-- until we managed to find ways of getting out from Lodz with all the belongings and across the boundary. Lodz became part of the Third Reich, while the other part of Poland was called so-called Generalgouvernement, under Dr. Frank, who became the governor of the-- of Poland, supposed to be the free Poland. There was Polish spoken, Polish newspapers, but was not Polish. It was entirely a-- controlled by Germans with the influence and Germanistic feeling. The other part of Poland was annexed as Warthegau. And Lodz was included in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: By paying off and smuggling through the boundary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that? Do you remember leaving and escaping?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not precisely. But there was sometime in January that we got to the city of Strykow. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-R-Y-K-O-W. Strykow. And we're staying in Strykow for about a month with a Polish family, of course paying quite a bit of money for this and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Don't remember at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't bother with us. They knew that there were Jews-- that we were Jews who escaped from Lodz and they were taking advantage of it. So every time we needed something, there was additional amount of money to be paid for. And-- until mother, one day, became very, very brave and went out to the-- the marketplace. They knew that on Thursday was a market with many farmers from local farms were coming in. And she somehow arranged for the following week to be transported by this Polish farmer all the way to Lowicz, spelled L-O-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was together with you at this point? Who was with you-- with you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just me and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in-- already in Belgium--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --studying--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father? How come you didn't come with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he could not meet the possibility of arranged-- of arranged escape from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't he come with you when you first left?

speaker: interviewee
text: He decided to stay a day longer to make sure that we are-- that we crossed the boundary and so he would follow the following day. And the following day never came. And it was impossible then, even with the greatest amount of money to bribe Germans to get him over to this side. And he remained upon Lodz. What did I-- what did he die actually of is very, very hard for me to tell. We did not get the notification of his sickness. We just got the notification of his death. And-- from a-- a hospital in Lodz. I don't remember what was the name of hospital. And notification that he was buried on Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you went from this town--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lowicz, we went directly to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to-- to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several-- several days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: By a horse and buggy covered with hay and all the belongings on it. As long as you did not get into the buses or general transportation like trains, where the-- where Germans were looking for Jews, traveling on-- on unofficial businesses. And if they found anybody, they devastated them. So there were-- we knew about those things. And we're deciding to travel by horse and buggy with typical farmer's wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about people getting captured like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- the news were traveling from mouth to mouth. There was no other way of obtaining any information, except through the people who experience anything like it, or have seen people doing it to the others. But the people we find out that people generally would not try to scare somebody to undertake any such thing, because it was in the interests of the people to survive and survive together with-- with all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the information about [INAUDIBLE] as finding anybody on a train and throwing him during the running of a train off the train, they were accepted in a way that that's how the new Germany will behave. And I have witnessed the atrocities done to the poor people in Lodz standing in line. So I could very simply tell that this information were quite accurate. When did you get to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Warsaw finally at the beginning of March of 1940. On a rainy, rainy snow mix day, cold. By the way, glad that we reached Warsaw then. The movement in Warsaw with the-- within within the city was much easier than traveling to Warsaw through the city. There was quite large population and did the same thing. If you could-- if you were dressed as-- in civilian clothing, Germans could not tell who was Jew, who was not, unless you would really dress in typical [INAUDIBLE] Jewish garb. But the appearance on their faces are all typical Semitic features. Give up individual and the Germans started to question them. Especially when it came to the question of IDs and a way of communication.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have IDs for when you were in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Warsaw, we had to obtain ID.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's one of the-- the photos of-- that I had on my ID in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get an ID, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to register with the Jewish community in Warsaw. The Jewish community center was on Grzybowska street, spelled G-R-Z-Y-S-- Y-B-O-- B-O-W-S-K-A. In-- and the man in charge of Jewish community was Dr. [POLISH]. Once you got the ID, you had to expect the Germans would send you some notification to report to certain places of work. And there were possibility that people did not have to go to work if they could know how to pay for it. There was something like working talons-- talons, spelled T-A-L-O-N. Like a booklet of tickets that you had to surrender to the group of-- of the foremen at such place of work. And there was mostly military outposts in Warsaw. Like on the airport, you have no [INAUDIBLE] or-- or the damaged building that they wanted to convert for their own use.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do this kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young for-- at that time to get any talons. But I remember on several occasion, I want work. My members of my family could not go to such work, I took their talons to go to work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: First time I got to work, there was some infantry place. U-J-A-Z-D-- D-O-W-S-K, in Warsaw, clearing out debris from bombardments of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're living with part of our family, mother's family. Majority of mother's family is from Warsaw. There were some 56 members that I know of. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the sister of my-- my mother and Dzika, the street, spelled D-Z-I-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your-- does--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dzika in Polish means wild street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your aunt's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt was Rose Medzycki, spelled M-E-D-Z-Y-C-K-I. Medzycki. And they were receiving at the beginning a very nice one. And then we find that we're all in quarters renting a place from some people in Warsaw. In the District of Jewish quarters, and this was on [NON-ENGLISH] street in Warsaw, off Zamenhof Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four. Today-- there was-- prior, they used to call this Kupiecka street. K-U-P-I-E-C-K-A, Kupiecka, which means dealing street. Many deals were conducted over there. And today, it is [NON-ENGLISH] street. [INAUDIBLE]
